title: One Does Not Simply Meme Alone: Evaluating Co-Creativity Between LLMs and Humans in the Generation of Humor
venue: 30th International Conference on Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI ’25), March 24–27, 2025, Cagliari, Italy
research_questions: How does access to a conversational LLM humor assistant affect participants’ productivity, perceived workload, effort, and ownership during meme-caption ideation compared with human-only creation?
How do memes created by humans alone, through human-AI collaboration, and by an LLM alone compare in perceived humor, creativity, and shareability?

datasets_used.name: Popular meme templates and topics
datasets_used.size: six background images of popular memes and three topics (work, food, sports); one image was excluded from the study, leaving five remaining images for rating

datasets_used.name: Study-generated meme image corpus
datasets_used.size: 882 ideas marked as favorites across both human-involved study conditions; 415 baseline ideas and 441 collaborative ideas usable; after curation, 335 baseline images and 307 collaborative images; Figure 5 shows 300 AI-driven meme images; 150 images per condition were sampled for rating

methods_proposed.name: LLM-assisted meme ideation interface
methods_proposed.summary: The study interface let participants view a meme template, enter/edit/favorite caption ideas, and, in the collaboration condition, chat with GPT-4o for idea support. The system prompt constrained the assistant to be helpful and polite and to produce at most three ideas per response, while the current image was always sent to the model before user prompts.

methods_proposed.name: AI-only meme caption generation prompt
methods_proposed.summary: For the AI-only condition, the authors sent the image and prompted GPT-4o to “generate 20 meme captions for this <image> about the topic of <topic>,” with topics drawn from work, food, and sports and image descriptions limited to no more than 10 words.

humor_theories.detail: The related work states that meme humor often relies on incongruity and the juxtaposition of unexpected elements, but the user study operationalizes ratings of humor, creativity, and shareability rather than an incongruity-theory annotation scheme.

evaluation_methods.detail: Between-subject meme-creation user study recruited 124 Prolific participants, excluded 26 non-completions, and analyzed human-only versus human-AI creation with self-reported creativity questions, NASA-TLX, general interface/ideation questions, and logs of ideas and LLM interactions.

A second Prolific sample of N=100 participants, of whom 98 completed the task, rated randomly displayed meme images; each participant rated at least 50 images on humor, creativity, and shareability, with 150 images per generation condition in the rated sample.

headline_results.finding: LLM-supported participants generated significantly more meme ideas than baseline participants, while the paper reports an overall average of 6.1 ideas (sd: 3.2) during ideation.
headline_results.value: absolute count W = 12652, p < 0.001; average number of ideas W = 1519.5, p < 0.001

headline_results.finding: Overall Raw TLX workload did not significantly differ, but the TLX effort subscale was significantly lower for participants using the LLM.
headline_results.metric: Raw TLX t-test; Mann-Whitney-U test for effort subscale

headline_results.finding: Participants without LLM support reported a higher degree of ownership for the generated captions than participants using the LLM.

Across all topics, meme ratings differed significantly by generation condition, and pairwise comparisons showed that AI-only memes were consistently rated more positively than memes with human involvement, except that shareability between cooperative and pure AI creation was not significant.
headline_results.metric: ANOVA for Funny and Creative; Kruskal-Wallis test for Shareable
headline_results.dataset: rated meme sample, 150 images per condition

headline_results.finding: When analyzed by topic, the previously found significant differences appeared primarily in memes about work.
headline_results.value: Work topic — Funny: F = 7.1, p = 0.001; Creative: χ² = 11.22, p = 0.004; Shareable: χ² = 8.470, p = 0.014

key_findings: GPT-4o assistance increased the number of ideas participants produced during meme ideation and lowered perceived effort, without increasing overall NASA-TLX workload.
Human-AI collaboration did not produce memes rated significantly better than human-only memes on humor, creativity, or shareability.
On average, AI-only memes were rated more positively than both human-only and human-AI collaborative memes across the three rating dimensions.

The top-performing memes showed a different pattern: human-created memes were strongest among the top humor examples, while human-AI collaborations stood out among top creativity and shareability examples.
Participants using the LLM reported a lower sense that the generated captions were their own ideas, though the paper says they generally still felt ownership.
The significant rating differences were driven mainly by the work-topic memes rather than evenly across work, sports, and food.
limitations: The study investigated only short-term, single-session interaction with the AI system, so it does not show how prolonged use would affect creativity, satisfaction, skills, reliance on AI, or ownership.
Many participants did not fully use the LLM’s potential; less than half interacted with the LLM multiple times, and only six participants had more than eight interactions.
The chatbox interface was open-ended and lacked structure to guide iterative creative collaboration, which may have limited deeper engagement and idea revision.
Participants and raters came from varied cultural and social backgrounds, but the study did not systematically collect detailed demographic information such as ethnicity or language proficiency.
Using random crowdsourcing-platform evaluators may not capture subtle differences in humor appreciation across specific demographic groups.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that participants gave informed consent and were compensated equivalent to 15 USD for meme creation and 10 USD for meme rating. It also notes authorship and bias in AI training as ethical considerations in related work and reports that LLM assistance reduced perceived ownership of captions.

artifacts.other: The paper states that the full prompts and a full list of generated captions are available in the supplementary material.

human_samples.participant_count: 124 recruited; 26 excluded for non-completion; 98 with demographics reported after exclusions; per-condition completed counts for human-only vs human-AI are not reported
human_samples.population: Prolific participants with good English skills and prior use of an LLM interface at least once
human_samples.recruitment: Online platform Prolific; participant count determined after an initial power analysis
human_samples.observations: Each participant completed three meme-caption tasks using different image/topic combinations, spent at least 4 and at most 5 minutes on ideation per task, selected top three favorite ideas per task, created captioned meme images, and completed experience surveys including self-assessed creativity, NASA-TLX, interface and ideation questions. Across the two human-involved conditions, the study gathered 882 favorite ideas; 415 baseline and 441 collaborative ideas were usable after technical problems; after curation and excluding one meme template, 335 baseline images and 307 collaborative images remained.
human_samples.detail: Participants were assigned to a human-only baseline or a human-AI collaboration condition with access to GPT-4o through a chat interface. Reported demographics after exclusions: 63 male, 35 female; mean age 28.8 years, sd 8.7. Compensation was equivalent to 15 USD.

human_samples.population: Second Prolific sample with the same English-language prerequisites; prior LLM knowledge was not required
human_samples.observations: Participants rated a random sample of 50 meme images each on humor/funniness, creativity, and shareability. The rated pool contained 450 images: 150 human-only, 150 human-AI collaboration, and 150 AI-only images. Exact per-image rating counts are not reported.
human_samples.detail: Participants were reported as equally split between identifying as male and female, with average age 32.6 years, sd 11.1. Compensation was equivalent to 10 USD.

judges.name: Prolific meme raters
judges.detail: The second participant sample provided the human ratings that determined meme-quality results for humor/funniness, creativity, and shareability. The paper does not explicitly state whether generation condition was hidden from raters.

judges.name: Meme-creation participants
judges.detail: Participants in the human-only and human-AI conditions provided self-report outcomes such as NASA-TLX workload, perceived effort, perceived number of ideas, and ownership of generated captions. They necessarily knew whether they had access to the LLM assistant.

judges.name: Server logs and automatic idea extraction
judges.detail: The system recorded ideas, images, and LLM interactions; LLM responses in the collaboration interface were automatically processed to determine whether they contained ideas and to extract those ideas into the idea list. These logs supported productivity measures such as number of ideas.

judges.name: Paper authors / study team
judges.detail: The authors regenerated captioned images for consistent text placement and curated the generated images, excluding images where captions clearly did not match the task or where caption length obscured most of the image. The paper does not report multiple curators, blinding, or inter-rater agreement for this curation.

generation_selection.n: Variable number of participant-generated ideas during a 4–5 minute ideation period per task; participants then selected their top three ideas per task
generation_selection.detail: Participants generated captions without AI assistance, selected favorite ideas, and created final meme images. For evaluation, after technical filtering, regeneration, curation, and exclusion of one image template, the authors randomly sampled 10 images for each remaining image/topic combination, yielding 150 rated human-only images.

generation_selection.n: Variable number of human and LLM-assisted ideas during a 4–5 minute ideation period per task; the system prompt constrained the LLM to at most three ideas per response; participants selected their top three ideas per task
generation_selection.detail: Participants could freely prompt GPT-4o through a chat interface during ideation, while the current image was always sent to the model before user prompts. Favorite selection and final image creation were performed by the participant without LLM support. For evaluation, after filtering, regeneration, curation, and exclusion of one image template, the authors randomly sampled 10 images for each remaining image/topic combination, yielding 150 rated collaborative images.

generation_selection.n: 20 captions per image/topic combination; 15 image/topic combinations; Figure 5 reports 300 AI-driven meme images before sampling, with 150 sampled for rating
generation_selection.detail: The model was sent the image and prompted to “generate 20 meme captions for this <image> about the topic of <topic>,” where the topic was work, food, or sports and the image description was no more than 10 words. Selection for evaluation was random rather than quality-based: 10 images per image/topic combination were sampled.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: The paper states that the study used the OpenAI API for GPT-4o in the human-AI chat interface and for the AI-only meme-caption condition, but it does not report an API snapshot/version or inference dates.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4o chat assistant in the human-AI collaboration interface
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Variable and user-initiated; exact calls per task, per participant, or per image are not reported
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: At most three ideas per LLM response by system-prompt constraint; total LLM suggestions per task are not reported
inference_budgets.detail: Participants could enter any prompt. The system prompt set the assistant context, required a helpful and polite tone, and limited each response to at most three ideas. The current image was always sent to the LLM before any user prompt. The paper reports in limitations that less than half of participants interacted with the LLM multiple times and only six had more than eight interactions, but it does not provide full interaction-count distributions.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Not explicitly reported; the paper says the model was prompted for each image/topic combination
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 20 captions per image/topic combination; 15 combinations; 300 AI-driven meme images before random sampling according to Figure 5
inference_budgets.detail: Prompt described in the paper: “generate 20 meme captions for this <image> about the topic of <topic>.” The <topic> was one of work, food, or sports; the <image> field was a brief description of no more than 10 words. No temperature, top-p, seed, max-token setting, latency, or API cost is reported.

human_baselines.scope: Productivity, workload, effort, and ownership in meme creation
human_baselines.description: A human-only baseline group completed the same three meme-captioning tasks without AI assistance and was compared with the human-AI collaboration group on idea counts, NASA-TLX, perceived effort, and ownership.
human_baselines.detail: The human-only and human-AI groups used the same task structure, time window, templates/topics sampling procedure, favorite-selection step, image editor, and post-task survey, with LLM access as the key manipulated difference. Exact completed sample sizes per condition are not reported.

human_baselines.scope: Rated quality of generated memes versus GPT-4o AI-only memes
human_baselines.description: Human-only memes were included as a baseline against human-AI collaboration and AI-only GPT-4o memes in a shared human-rating survey of humor/funniness, creativity, and shareability.
human_baselines.detail: The rating procedure used the same second rater pool, same metrics, same remaining five templates, same three topics, and 150 rated images per condition. However, generation and selection budgets were not materially matched: human and collaborative images came from timed participant ideation followed by participant top-three favorite selection and author curation, while AI-only images came from GPT-4o generating 20 captions per image/topic combination followed by random sampling; no matched time, prompt-count, or selection budget is reported.

mitigations: Meme captions were generated during the study rather than scored against a fixed public answer key.
Quality labels were newly collected from a Prolific rater sample.
The evaluation used the same sampled templates/topics and the same rater survey across conditions.
contamination_risk.basis: This is not a conventional public benchmark with fixed labels, so answer-key contamination is not the main issue. However, the task uses popular internet meme templates and broad topics, and GPT-4o is a proprietary model likely trained on large internet data containing meme templates, meme conventions, and possibly similar captions. The paper provides no decontamination test, API snapshot, or analysis of memorized/common captions. New human ratings reduce direct benchmark-label leakage, but training overlap with public meme culture remains a plausible influence on AI-only performance claims.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Meme-quality conclusions were determined by human Prolific ratings; productivity and workload conclusions were determined by server logs and human self-reports.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: GPT-4o was used to generate suggestions and AI-only captions, not to evaluate or score outputs. No LLM judge is reported.

reporting_gaps: Exact GPT-4o API snapshot/version is not reported.
Inference dates or access dates for GPT-4o are not reported.
Sampling parameters such as temperature, top-p, seed, and max tokens are not reported.
Exact number of GPT-4o calls per participant, task, image, or image/topic combination is not reported.
Token budgets, latency, compute usage, and OpenAI API cost are not reported.

Completed participant counts per human-only and human-AI condition are not reported; the abstract’s statement of three groups of 50 participants is not reconciled with the Methods report of 124 recruited and 26 excluded human participants plus an autonomous AI-only condition.
The rating scale format, range, and anchors for humor, creativity, and shareability are not specified in the supplied paper text.
Exact per-image rating counts and whether random image display was balanced across raters are not reported.
Whether meme raters were blind to generation condition is not explicitly reported.
The commercial survey platform used for subjective surveys and ratings is not named.
The automatic method for extracting ideas from LLM responses is not specified or validated.
Author curation procedures are underspecified: number of curators, blinding, adjudication, and inter-rater reliability are not reported.
The full system prompt and full list of generated captions are said to be in supplementary material but are not included in the supplied paper text.
Detailed demographic variables relevant to humor perception, such as ethnicity and language proficiency, were not systematically collected, according to the authors’ limitation section.
